lem:amplification-dual-norm.proof_tex: Proof. Identify a degree-beta polynomial r(u)=sum_{ell=0}^beta b_ell u^ell with its coefficient vector b. The linear map E sends b to the node values (r(p_0),...,r(p_k)); the endpoint contrast is L(b)=r(1)-r(0)=sum_{ell=1}^beta b_ell. A weight vector w represents L on all degree-beta polynomials exactly when E^T w is the coefficient vector of L, namely the moment equations defining W_beta(p). Thus inf_{w in W_beta(p)} sum_j |w_j| is the minimum l1 norm of a representing vector for L through E. The dual norm identity for finite-dimensional l1/l_infty duality gives this minimum as sup{L(b): ||Eb||_infty<=1}; replacing r by -r if needed gives the absolute-value form. Squaring the identity is exactly def:amplification-criterion.

snapshot.stmt: For a schedule p with distinct nodes and with W_beta(p) nonempty, inf_{w in W_beta(p)} sum_j |w_j| equals sup{|r(1)-r(0)| : r is a real polynomial of degree at most beta and max_j |r(p_j)|<=1}. Consequently A_beta(p) is the square of this dual norm.

defs.def:unbiased-weight-set: W_beta(p) = { w in R^(k+1) : sum_{j=0}^k w_j p_j^0 = 0 and sum_{j=0}^k w_j p_j^ell = 1 for ell = 1, ..., beta }
defs.def:amplification-criterion: A_beta(p) = inf_{w in W_beta(p)} (sum_{j=0}^k |w_j|)^2 and M_{beta,k,q} = inf_{p in S_{k,q}} A_beta(p).

node.statement: For a schedule p with distinct nodes and with W_beta(p) nonempty, inf_{w in W_beta(p)} sum_j |w_j| equals sup{|r(1)-r(0)| : r is a real polynomial of degree at most beta and max_j |r(p_j)|<=1}. Consequently A_beta(p) is the square of this dual norm.
node.proof_tex: Proof. Identify a degree-beta polynomial r(u)=sum_{ell=0}^beta b_ell u^ell with its coefficient vector b. The linear map E sends b to the node values (r(p_0),...,r(p_k)); the endpoint contrast is L(b)=r(1)-r(0)=sum_{ell=1}^beta b_ell. A weight vector w represents L on all degree-beta polynomials exactly when E^T w is the coefficient vector of L, namely the moment equations defining W_beta(p). Thus inf_{w in W_beta(p)} sum_j |w_j| is the minimum l1 norm of a representing vector for L through E. The dual norm identity for finite-dimensional l1/l_infty duality gives this minimum as sup{L(b): ||Eb||_infty<=1}; replacing r by -r if needed gives the absolute-value form. Squaring the identity is exactly def:amplification-criterion.

lem:oversampled-chebyshev-lobatto-normin.proof_tex: Proof. Put Q(theta)=R(-cos theta). Then Q is an even trigonometric polynomial of degree at most beta on [0,2pi], and the displayed nodes are the equally spaced samples theta_j=pi j/k together with their even reflection, equivalently the 2k-point grid on the circle. Because beta/k <= 1/c < 1, choose an integer bandwidth m with beta < m < k and with both m/k and beta/m bounded away from 1 by constants depending only on c. Let V be the de la Vallee Poussin trigonometric kernel whose Fourier multiplier is one on frequencies |ell|<=beta, tapers linearly, and vanishes for |ell|>=m. The discrete Fourier orthogonality on the 2k grid is exact for degree less than k, so Q(theta) = (1/(2k)) sum_{r=0}^{2k-1} Q(theta_r) V(theta-theta_r). The normalized discrete L1 norm (1/(2k)) sum_r |V(theta-theta_r)| is bounded uniformly in theta by a constant K(c); this is the standard de la Vallee Poussin L1 bound, with the constant depending only on the oversampling gap between beta and k. Taking suprema gives ||Q||_infty <= K(c) max_r |Q(theta_r)|. Since Q is even, max over the 2k grid equals max_{0<=j<=k}|R(-cos(pi j/k))|, proving the claim.

snapshot.stmt: For each c>1 there is a finite constant K(c) such that, for every beta>=1, every integer k>=c beta, and every real polynomial R of degree at most beta, sup_{x in [-1,1]} |R(x)| <= K(c) max_{0<=j<=k} |R(-cos(pi j/k))|.

defs.def:chebyshev-schedule: p_j^Ch(k,q) = q { 1 - cos(pi j / k) } / 2 for j = 0, ..., k, and p^Ch(k,q) = (p_0^Ch(k,q), ..., p_k^Ch(k,q)).

node.statement: For each c>1 there is a finite constant K(c) such that, for every beta>=1, every integer k>=c beta, and every real polynomial R of degree at most beta, sup_{x in [-1,1]} |R(x)| <= K(c) max_{0<=j<=k} |R(-cos(pi j/k))|.
node.proof_tex: Proof. Put Q(theta)=R(-cos theta). Then Q is an even trigonometric polynomial of degree at most beta on [0,2pi], and the displayed nodes are the equally spaced samples theta_j=pi j/k together with their even reflection, equivalently the 2k-point grid on the circle. Because beta/k <= 1/c < 1, choose an integer bandwidth m with beta < m < k and with both m/k and beta/m bounded away from 1 by constants depending only on c. Let V be the de la Vallee Poussin trigonometric kernel whose Fourier multiplier is one on frequencies |ell|<=beta, tapers linearly, and vanishes for |ell|>=m. The discrete Fourier orthogonality on the 2k grid is exact for degree less than k, so Q(theta) = (1/(2k)) sum_{r=0}^{2k-1} Q(theta_r) V(theta-theta_r). The normalized discrete L1 norm (1/(2k)) sum_r |V(theta-theta_r)| is bounded uniformly in theta by a constant K(c); this is the standard de la Vallee Poussin L1 bound, with the constant depending only on the oversampling gap between beta and k. Taking suprema gives ||Q||_infty <= K(c) max_r |Q(theta_r)|. Since Q is even, max over the 2k grid equals max_{0<=j<=k}|R(-cos(pi j/k))|, proving the claim.

lem:continuous-chebyshev-endpoint-bound.proof_tex: Proof. The Chebyshev extremal inequality states that, among real polynomials of degree at most beta bounded by one on [-1,1], the maximum possible absolute value at x_q>1 is T_beta(x_q). Hence |R(x_q)-R(-1)| <= |R(x_q)|+|R(-1)| <= T_beta(x_q)+1. Writing x_q=cosh a with a>0 gives lambda(q)=e^a and T_beta(x_q)=cosh(beta a)=(lambda(q)^beta+lambda(q)^(-beta))/2. Since q<=q_max<1, lambda(q)>=lambda(q_max)>1, so T_beta(x_q)+1 <= C(q_max) lambda(q)^beta. For the lower order, take R=T_beta itself. It is bounded by one on [-1,1], and |T_beta(x_q)-T_beta(-1)| is at least T_beta(x_q)-1. The ratio (T_beta(x_q)-1)/lambda(q)^beta is positive for every beta>=1 and q in (0,q_max], tends to 1/2 as beta grows uniformly away from q=1, and has a positive minimum over the finite remaining beta values; call that minimum c(q_max). This proves the two-sided endpoint order.

snapshot.stmt: Let q in (0,1), x_q=2/q-1, lambda(q)=x_q+sqrt(x_q^2-1), and rho(q)=q lambda(q)=(1+sqrt(1-q))^2. For every degree-beta polynomial R with sup_{x in [-1,1]}|R(x)|<=1, |R(x_q)-R(-1)| <= C(q_max) lambda(q)^beta uniformly over q in (0,q_max]; and the Chebyshev polynomial T_beta gives the matching lower order T_beta(x_q)-1 >= c(q_max) lambda(q)^beta.

node.statement: Let q in (0,1), x_q=2/q-1, lambda(q)=x_q+sqrt(x_q^2-1), and rho(q)=q lambda(q)=(1+sqrt(1-q))^2. For every degree-beta polynomial R with sup_{x in [-1,1]}|R(x)|<=1, |R(x_q)-R(-1)| <= C(q_max) lambda(q)^beta uniformly over q in (0,q_max]; and the Chebyshev polynomial T_beta gives the matching lower order T_beta(x_q)-1 >= c(q_max) lambda(q)^beta.
node.proof_tex: Proof. The Chebyshev extremal inequality states that, among real polynomials of degree at most beta bounded by one on [-1,1], the maximum possible absolute value at x_q>1 is T_beta(x_q). Hence |R(x_q)-R(-1)| <= |R(x_q)|+|R(-1)| <= T_beta(x_q)+1. Writing x_q=cosh a with a>0 gives lambda(q)=e^a and T_beta(x_q)=cosh(beta a)=(lambda(q)^beta+lambda(q)^(-beta))/2. Since q<=q_max<1, lambda(q)>=lambda(q_max)>1, so T_beta(x_q)+1 <= C(q_max) lambda(q)^beta. For the lower order, take R=T_beta itself. It is bounded by one on [-1,1], and |T_beta(x_q)-T_beta(-1)| is at least T_beta(x_q)-1. The ratio (T_beta(x_q)-1)/lambda(q)^beta is positive for every beta>=1 and q in (0,q_max], tends to 1/2 as beta grows uniformly away from q=1, and has a positive minimum over the finite remaining beta values; call that minimum c(q_max). This proves the two-sided endpoint order.

lem:chebyshev-schedule-admissible.proof_tex: Proof. By def:chebyshev-schedule, p_j^Ch(k,q)=q(1-cos(pi j/k))/2. At j=0 this is q(1-1)/2=0, and at j=k this is q(1-(-1))/2=q. For 0<=j<l<=k, pi j/k<pi l/k and cosine is strictly decreasing on [0,pi], hence 1-cos(pi j/k)<1-cos(pi l/k). Multiplication by q/2>0 preserves the strict inequality. Therefore 0=p_0^Ch<p_1^Ch<...<p_k^Ch=q, which is exactly membership in S_{k,q}.

snapshot.stmt: For every integer k>=1 and q in (0,1], the shifted Chebyshev-Lobatto schedule p^Ch(k,q) belongs to S_{k,q}.

defs.def:chebyshev-schedule: p_j^Ch(k,q) = q { 1 - cos(pi j / k) } / 2 for j = 0, ..., k, and p^Ch(k,q) = (p_0^Ch(k,q), ..., p_k^Ch(k,q)).

node.statement: For every integer k>=1 and q in (0,1], the shifted Chebyshev-Lobatto schedule p^Ch(k,q) belongs to S_{k,q}.
node.proof_tex: Proof. By def:chebyshev-schedule, p_j^Ch(k,q)=q(1-cos(pi j/k))/2. At j=0 this is q(1-1)/2=0, and at j=k this is q(1-(-1))/2=q. For 0<=j<l<=k, pi j/k<pi l/k and cosine is strictly decreasing on [0,pi], hence 1-cos(pi j/k)<1-cos(pi l/k). Multiplication by q/2>0 preserves the strict inequality. Therefore 0=p_0^Ch<p_1^Ch<...<p_k^Ch=q, which is exactly membership in S_{k,q}.

lem:unbiased-weight-set-nonempty.proof_tex: Proof. Fix beta >= 1, k >= beta, and p in S_{k,q}. By def:budgeted-schedule-class, p_0=0 and 0<p_1<...<p_k=q, so the k+1 nodes p_0,...,p_k are distinct. Let B be the (beta+1) by (k+1) moment matrix B_{ell j}=p_j^ell for ell=0,...,beta and j=0,...,k, with the convention p_j^0=1. We show that B has full row rank. If alpha=(alpha_0,...,alpha_beta) satisfies alpha^T B=0, then the polynomial h(u)=sum_{ell=0}^beta alpha_ell u^ell obeys h(p_j)=0 for all j=0,...,k. Since k+1 >= beta+1 and the p_j are distinct, h has at least beta+1 distinct zeros. A nonzero real polynomial of degree at most beta cannot have beta+1 distinct zeros, hence h is the zero polynomial and alpha=0. Thus the rows of B are linearly independent, so B has row rank beta+1 and the linear map w -> Bw from R^{k+1} onto R^{beta+1} is surjective. Taking the target moment vector b=(0,1,1,...,1) in R^{beta+1}, there exists w with Bw=b. By def:unbiased-weight-set this w belongs to W_beta(p). Therefore W_beta(p) is nonempty for every p in S_{k,q}.

snapshot.stmt: Assume beta>=1 and k>=beta. For every schedule p in S_{k,q}, the unbiased linear weight set W_beta(p) is nonempty.

defs.def:unbiased-weight-set: W_beta(p) = { w in R^(k+1) : sum_{j=0}^k w_j p_j^0 = 0 and sum_{j=0}^k w_j p_j^ell = 1 for ell = 1, ..., beta }

node.statement: Assume beta>=1 and k>=beta. For every schedule p in S_{k,q}, the unbiased linear weight set W_beta(p) is nonempty.
node.proof_tex: Proof. Fix beta >= 1, k >= beta, and p in S_{k,q}. By def:budgeted-schedule-class, p_0=0 and 0<p_1<...<p_k=q, so the k+1 nodes p_0,...,p_k are distinct. Let B be the (beta+1) by (k+1) moment matrix B_{ell j}=p_j^ell for ell=0,...,beta and j=0,...,k, with the convention p_j^0=1. We show that B has full row rank. If alpha=(alpha_0,...,alpha_beta) satisfies alpha^T B=0, then the polynomial h(u)=sum_{ell=0}^beta alpha_ell u^ell obeys h(p_j)=0 for all j=0,...,k. Since k+1 >= beta+1 and the p_j are distinct, h has at least beta+1 distinct zeros. A nonzero real polynomial of degree at most beta cannot have beta+1 distinct zeros, hence h is the zero polynomial and alpha=0. Thus the rows of B are linearly independent, so B has row rank beta+1 and the linear map w -> Bw from R^{k+1} onto R^{beta+1} is surjective. Taking the target moment vector b=(0,1,1,...,1) in R^{beta+1}, there exists w with Bw=b. By def:unbiased-weight-set this w belongs to W_beta(p). Therefore W_beta(p) is nonempty for every p in S_{k,q}.

lem:variance-envelope-sharpness.proof_tex: Proof. Let X_0,...,X_k be square-integrable under the rollout randomization pi and suppose Var_pi(X_j) <= sigma_0^2/n for every j. For any real weights w, write X_j^c=X_j-E_pi X_j. The L2(pi) triangle inequality gives ||sum_{j=0}^k w_j X_j^c||_2 <= sum_{j=0}^k |w_j| ||X_j^c||_2. Squaring and using ||X_j^c||_2^2=Var_pi(X_j) <= sigma_0^2/n yields Var_pi(sum_{j=0}^k w_j X_j) <= (sigma_0^2/n)(sum_{j=0}^k |w_j|)^2. For sharpness of the covariance envelope, consider the class of positive semidefinite covariance matrices Gamma with Gamma_{jj} <= sigma_0^2/n. The same argument gives w' Gamma w <= (sigma_0^2/n)(sum_j |w_j|)^2. Conversely, with s_j=sign(w_j), taking Gamma=(sigma_0^2/n) ss' gives a positive semidefinite rank-one covariance matrix with diagonal sigma_0^2/n and w' Gamma w=(sigma_0^2/n)(sum_j |w_j|)^2. Thus the total-variation round-variance envelope assigns exactly this worst-case variance to the weight vector w.

snapshot.stmt: If X_0,...,X_k are square-integrable rollout-round statistics with Var_pi(X_j)<=sigma_0^2/n for every j, then Var_pi(sum_j w_j X_j) <= sigma_0^2 n^(-1) (sum_j |w_j|)^2 for every real w. Moreover this bound is sharp over covariance matrices whose diagonal entries are bounded by sigma_0^2/n.

assumptions.ass:round-mean-variance-envelope: For every law P, every admissible schedule p, and every rollout measurement j, Var_pi(bar_Y_j) <= sigma_0^2 / n.

node.statement: If X_0,...,X_k are square-integrable rollout-round statistics with Var_pi(X_j)<=sigma_0^2/n for every j, then Var_pi(sum_j w_j X_j) <= sigma_0^2 n^(-1) (sum_j |w_j|)^2 for every real w. Moreover this bound is sharp over covariance matrices whose diagonal entries are bounded by sigma_0^2/n.
node.proof_tex: Proof. Let X_0,...,X_k be square-integrable under the rollout randomization pi and suppose Var_pi(X_j) <= sigma_0^2/n for every j. For any real weights w, write X_j^c=X_j-E_pi X_j. The L2(pi) triangle inequality gives ||sum_{j=0}^k w_j X_j^c||_2 <= sum_{j=0}^k |w_j| ||X_j^c||_2. Squaring and using ||X_j^c||_2^2=Var_pi(X_j) <= sigma_0^2/n yields Var_pi(sum_{j=0}^k w_j X_j) <= (sigma_0^2/n)(sum_{j=0}^k |w_j|)^2. For sharpness of the covariance envelope, consider the class of positive semidefinite covariance matrices Gamma with Gamma_{jj} <= sigma_0^2/n. The same argument gives w' Gamma w <= (sigma_0^2/n)(sum_j |w_j|)^2. Conversely, with s_j=sign(w_j), taking Gamma=(sigma_0^2/n) ss' gives a positive semidefinite rank-one covariance matrix with diagonal sigma_0^2/n and w' Gamma w=(sigma_0^2/n)(sum_j |w_j|)^2. Thus the total-variation round-variance envelope assigns exactly this worst-case variance to the weight vector w.

lem:exact-risk-envelope-upper.proof_tex: Fix beta>=1, k>=beta, q in (0,1], and p in S_{k,q}. By lem:unbiased-weight-set-nonempty, W_beta(p) is nonempty. Fix w in W_beta(p) and P in P_beta. By def:rollout-law-class, P satisfies ass:round-mean-variance-envelope. Let X_j=bar_Y_j-E_pi bar_Y_j. Since Gamma_P(p) is the exact covariance matrix of (bar_Y_0,...,bar_Y_k), w' Gamma_P(p) w = Var_pi(sum_{j=0}^k w_j bar_Y_j) = ||sum_{j=0}^k w_j X_j||_2^2. The triangle inequality in L_2(pi) gives ||sum_j w_j X_j||_2 <= sum_j |w_j| ||X_j||_2 = sum_j |w_j| Var_pi(bar_Y_j)^(1/2). By ass:round-mean-variance-envelope, each Var_pi(bar_Y_j) <= sigma_0^2/n, hence w' Gamma_P(p) w <= (sigma_0^2/n)(sum_j |w_j|)^2. This bound is uniform in P in P_beta. Taking the supremum over P in P_beta, then the infimum over w in W_beta(p), gives inf_{w in W_beta(p)} sup_{P in P_beta} w' Gamma_P(p) w <= (sigma_0^2/n) inf_{w in W_beta(p)}(sum_j |w_j|)^2 = (sigma_0^2/n) A_beta(p). Taking the infimum over p in S_{k,q} and using def:exact-nested-risk and def:amplification-criterion gives R_exact(beta,k,q) <= (sigma_0^2/n) M_{beta,k,q}.

snapshot.stmt: Assume beta>=1 and k>=beta. For every q in (0,1] and every p in S_{k,q}, inf_{w in W_beta(p)} sup_{P in P_beta} w' Gamma_P(p) w <= (sigma_0^2/n) A_beta(p). Consequently R_exact(beta,k,q) <= (sigma_0^2/n) M_{beta,k,q}.

defs.def:rollout-law-class: P_beta = { P : ass:static-rollout-consistency and ass:beta-order-polynomial and ass:round-mean-variance-envelope }
defs.def:exact-nested-risk: R_exact(beta,k,q) = inf_{p in S_{k,q}} inf_{w in W_beta(p)} sup_{P in P_beta} w' Gamma_P(p) w.
defs.def:amplification-criterion: A_beta(p) = inf_{w in W_beta(p)} (sum_{j=0}^k |w_j|)^2 and M_{beta,k,q} = inf_{p in S_{k,q}} A_beta(p).
defs.def:unbiased-weight-set: W_beta(p) = { w in R^(k+1) : sum_{j=0}^k w_j p_j^0 = 0 and sum_{j=0}^k w_j p_j^ell = 1 for ell = 1, ..., beta }

assumptions.ass:round-mean-variance-envelope: For every law P, every admissible schedule p, and every rollout measurement j, Var_pi(bar_Y_j) <= sigma_0^2 / n.

node.statement: Assume beta>=1 and k>=beta. For every q in (0,1] and every p in S_{k,q}, inf_{w in W_beta(p)} sup_{P in P_beta} w' Gamma_P(p) w <= (sigma_0^2/n) A_beta(p). Consequently R_exact(beta,k,q) <= (sigma_0^2/n) M_{beta,k,q}.
node.proof_tex: Fix beta>=1, k>=beta, q in (0,1], and p in S_{k,q}. By lem:unbiased-weight-set-nonempty, W_beta(p) is nonempty. Fix w in W_beta(p) and P in P_beta. By def:rollout-law-class, P satisfies ass:round-mean-variance-envelope. Let X_j=bar_Y_j-E_pi bar_Y_j. Since Gamma_P(p) is the exact covariance matrix of (bar_Y_0,...,bar_Y_k), w' Gamma_P(p) w = Var_pi(sum_{j=0}^k w_j bar_Y_j) = ||sum_{j=0}^k w_j X_j||_2^2. The triangle inequality in L_2(pi) gives ||sum_j w_j X_j||_2 <= sum_j |w_j| ||X_j||_2 = sum_j |w_j| Var_pi(bar_Y_j)^(1/2). By ass:round-mean-variance-envelope, each Var_pi(bar_Y_j) <= sigma_0^2/n, hence w' Gamma_P(p) w <= (sigma_0^2/n)(sum_j |w_j|)^2. This bound is uniform in P in P_beta. Taking the supremum over P in P_beta, then the infimum over w in W_beta(p), gives inf_{w in W_beta(p)} sup_{P in P_beta} w' Gamma_P(p) w <= (sigma_0^2/n) inf_{w in W_beta(p)}(sum_j |w_j|)^2 = (sigma_0^2/n) A_beta(p). Taking the infimum over p in S_{k,q} and using def:exact-nested-risk and def:amplification-criterion gives R_exact(beta,k,q) <= (sigma_0^2/n) M_{beta,k,q}.

prop:rollout-polynomial-identity.proof_tex: Proof. Fix a law P satisfying the static rollout consistency and beta-order polynomial assumptions. The causal estimand is tau_P = m_P(1) - m_P(0). By ass:beta-order-polynomial, for every u in [0,1], m_P(u) = sum_{ell=0}^beta a_{P,ell} u^ell. Evaluating at u=1 gives m_P(1) = sum_{ell=0}^beta a_{P,ell}. Evaluating at u=0 gives m_P(0) = a_{P,0}, since 0^ell = 0 for ell >= 1 and the constant term is a_{P,0}. Therefore tau_P = m_P(1)-m_P(0) = (sum_{ell=0}^beta a_{P,ell}) - a_{P,0} = sum_{ell=1}^beta a_{P,ell}. Static rollout consistency supplies the causal interpretation of bar_Y_j and m_P as the design expectation of the observed rollout mean under the contemporaneous assignment; the algebraic endpoint identity itself is the polynomial evaluation just displayed.

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:static-rollout-consistency and ass:beta-order-polynomial, tau_P = m_P(1) - m_P(0) = sum_{ell=1}^beta a_{P,ell}.

assumptions.ass:static-rollout-consistency: At each rollout measurement j, the observed round mean equals bar_Y_j = n^(-1) sum_{i in U_n} Y_i(Z_j), and the potential outcomes depend on the contemporaneous assignment vector Z_j but not on earlier rollout steps.
assumptions.ass:beta-order-polynomial: For every law P, the rollout mean curve satisfies m_P(u) = sum_{ell=0}^beta a_{P,ell} u^ell for all u in [0,1].

thm:tv-envelope-design.proof_tex:
Proof. Assume beta>=1 and k>=beta. Fix p in S_{k,q}, w in W_beta(p), and P in P_beta. By def:rollout-law-class, P satisfies ass:static-rollout-consistency, ass:beta-order-polynomial, and ass:round-mean-variance-envelope. Since the schedule p is fixed by design, the definition of the rollout mean curve gives E_pi[bar_Y_j]=m_P(p_j) for each measurement j. Therefore E_pi[hat_tau_{w,p}]=sum_{j=0}^k w_j m_P(p_j). By ass:beta-order-polynomial, m_P(u)=sum_{ell=0}^beta a_{P,ell} u^ell on [0,1]. Substituting this polynomial expansion and interchanging finite sums gives E_pi[hat_tau_{w,p}]=sum_{ell=0}^beta a_{P,ell} sum_{j=0}^k w_j p_j^ell. By def:unbiased-weight-set, sum_j w_j p_j^0=0 and sum_j w_j p_j^ell=1 for ell=1,...,beta. Hence E_pi[hat_tau_{w,p}]=sum_{ell=1}^beta a_{P,ell}. By prop:rollout-polynomial-identity, sum_{ell=1}^beta a_{P,ell}=tau_P. Thus hat_tau_{w,p} is design-unbiased for tau_P over P_beta.

For the variance bound, apply lem:variance-envelope-sharpness with X_j=bar_Y_j. The variance premise Var_pi(bar_Y_j) <= sigma_0^2/n is exactly ass:round-mean-variance-envelope for P in P_beta and the admissible schedule p. Hence Var_pi(hat_tau_{w,p})=Var_pi(sum_j w_j bar_Y_j) <= sigma_0^2 n^{-1} (sum_{j=0}^k |w_j|)^2.

It remains to justify the optimization consequences. By lem:unbiased-weight-set-nonempty, W_beta(p) is nonempty for every p in S_{k,q}. For a fixed schedule p, the sharp total-variation variance envelope from lem:variance-envelope-sharpness assigns the weight vector w the worst-case design variance (sigma_0^2/n)(sum_j |w_j|)^2. Minimizing over the feasible linear-unbiased weights therefore gives the fixed-schedule envelope value (sigma_0^2/n) inf_{w in W_beta(p)} (sum_j |w_j|)^2. By def:amplification-criterion this is (sigma_0^2/n) A_beta(p). In finite dimension the infimum is an attained minimum: W_beta(p) is a nonempty closed affine set, and after choosing any feasible w^0 one may restrict to the compact l1 ball {w in W_beta(p): sum_j |w_j| <= sum_j |w^0_j|}. Thus A_beta(p) is the best achievable fixed-schedule amplification value. Taking the infimum over p in S_{k,q} gives, again by def:amplification-criterion, the dimensionless minimax amplification M_{beta,k,q}=inf_{p in S_{k,q}} A_beta(p), and multiplying by sigma_0^2/n gives the corresponding minimax design variance (sigma_0^2/n) M_{beta,k,q}. Finally, if a schedule p^* in S_{k,q} attains A_beta(p^*)=M_{beta,k,q}, then p^* is by definition an optimal schedule for the total-variation variance envelope.

snapshot.stmt: Assume beta>=1 and k>=beta. For every schedule p in S_{k,q} and every weight vector w in W_beta(p), the estimator hat_tau_{w,p} is design-unbiased for tau_P over P in P_beta, and Var_pi(hat_tau_{w,p}) <= sigma_0^2 n^(-1) (sum_{j=0}^k |w_j|)^2. Consequently, since W_beta(p) is nonempty for every p in S_{k,q}, over the sharp total-variation variance envelope the best achievable design variance for a fixed schedule p is sigma_0^2 n^(-1) A_beta(p); the dimensionless minimax amplification value over budgeted schedules is M_{beta,k,q}=inf_{p in S_{k,q}} A_beta(p); the corresponding minimax design variance over budgeted schedules is sigma_0^2 n^(-1) M_{beta,k,q}; and an optimal schedule for the variance envelope is any minimizer p^* in S_{k,q} attaining A_beta(p^*)=M_{beta,k,q}.

defs.def:unbiased-weight-set: W_beta(p) = { w in R^(k+1) : sum_{j=0}^k w_j p_j^0 = 0 and sum_{j=0}^k w_j p_j^ell = 1 for ell = 1, ..., beta }
defs.def:amplification-criterion: A_beta(p) = inf_{w in W_beta(p)} (sum_{j=0}^k |w_j|)^2 and M_{beta,k,q} = inf_{p in S_{k,q}} A_beta(p).
defs.def:rollout-law-class: P_beta = { P : ass:static-rollout-consistency and ass:beta-order-polynomial and ass:round-mean-variance-envelope }

assumptions.ass:round-mean-variance-envelope: For every law P, every admissible schedule p, and every rollout measurement j, Var_pi(bar_Y_j) <= sigma_0^2 / n.

prop:no-extrapolation-boundary.proof_tex:
Proof. Work at the full-budget boundary q=1 and take the equal-spacing schedule p^{eq}(\beta,1)=(0,1/\beta,2/\beta,\ldots,1). Thus p^{eq}_0=0 and p^{eq}_\beta=1. Define w\in\mathbb R^{\beta+1} by w_0=-1, w_\beta=1, and w_j=0 for 1\le j\le \beta-1; if \beta=1 the interior index set is empty.

We verify the defining moment equations of W_\beta(p^{eq}(\beta,1)). For the constant monomial, p_j^0=1 for every j in the notation of def:unbiased-weight-set, so
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{\beta} w_j (p^{eq}_j)^0
=\sum_{j=0}^{\beta} w_j
=-1+1=0.
\]
For any integer \ell with 1\le \ell\le \beta, all interior weights vanish, while (p^{eq}_0)^\ell=0^\ell=0 and (p^{eq}_\beta)^\ell=1^\ell=1. Hence
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{\beta} w_j (p^{eq}_j)^\ell
= - (p^{eq}_0)^\ell + (p^{eq}_\beta)^\ell
= -0+1=1.
\]
These are exactly the equations in def:unbiased-weight-set, so w\in W_\beta(p^{eq}(\beta,1)).

By def:amplification-criterion,
\[
A_\beta(p^{eq}(\beta,1))
= \inf_{v\in W_\beta(p^{eq}(\beta,1))}\Bigl(\sum_{j=0}^{\beta}|v_j|\Bigr)^2.
\]
Since the displayed endpoint vector w is feasible, the infimum is at most its value. Its total variation norm is
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{\beta}|w_j|=|-1|+|1|=2,
\]
so
\[
A_\beta(p^{eq}(\beta,1))\le 2^2=4.
\]
This proves both the membership claim and the stated no-extrapolation boundary bound.

snapshot.stmt: At the full-budget boundary q = 1, the equal-spacing schedule p^eq(beta,1) admits the endpoint rule w_0 = -1, w_beta = 1, and w_j = 0 for j = 1, ..., beta - 1; hence w belongs to W_beta(p^eq(beta,1)) and A_beta(p^eq(beta,1)) <= 4.

defs.def:unbiased-weight-set: W_beta(p) = { w in R^(k+1) : sum_{j=0}^k w_j p_j^0 = 0 and sum_{j=0}^k w_j p_j^ell = 1 for ell = 1, ..., beta }
defs.def:amplification-criterion: A_beta(p) = inf_{w in W_beta(p)} (sum_{j=0}^k |w_j|)^2 and M_{beta,k,q} = inf_{p in S_{k,q}} A_beta(p).

prop:equal-spacing-benchmark.proof_tex: Let beta >= 1 and q in (0,1]. Write the equal grid as p_j = q j / beta for j=0,...,beta. First suppose 0 < q < 1 and put x = 1/q > 1. Let L_j be the Lagrange basis polynomial on the normalized grid t_j = j/beta, j=0,...,beta: L_j(t) = product_{m != j} (t - m/beta)/(j/beta - m/beta). For any real polynomial r of degree at most beta, set R(t)=r(qt). Lagrange interpolation gives R(x)=sum_{j=0}^beta L_j(x) R(j/beta), and R(0)=r(0). Hence r(1)-r(0)=sum_{j=0}^beta (L_j(1/q)-1_{j=0}) r(qj/beta). Define w_j=L_j(1/q)-1_{j=0}. Testing the last identity on r(u)=u^ell shows sum_j w_j p_j^0=0 and sum_j w_j p_j^ell=1 for ell=1,...,beta, so w belongs to W_beta(p^eq(beta,q)) by def:unbiased-weight-set. Put y=beta/q. Since q<1, y>beta. For each j, |L_j(1/q)| = product_{m != j} |y-m|/|j-m| <= y^beta/(j!(beta-j)!). Therefore sum_j |L_j(1/q)| <= y^beta sum_j 1/(j!(beta-j)!) = y^beta 2^beta/beta! <= 2 y^beta for beta>=1. Since y^beta >= 1, sum_j |w_j| <= sum_j |L_j(1/q)| + 1 <= 3 (beta/q)^beta. By def:amplification-criterion, A_beta(p^eq(beta,q)) <= (sum_j |w_j|)^2 <= 9 (beta/q)^(2 beta). At q=1, take endpoint weights w_0=-1, w_beta=1, and w_j=0 for 1<=j<=beta-1. The constant moment is -1+1=0, and for every ell=1,...,beta the moment is -0^ell+1^ell=1; hence w belongs to W_beta(p^eq(beta,1)). Thus A_beta(p^eq(beta,1)) <= (|w_0|+|w_beta|)^2 = 4 <= 9 beta^(2 beta) = 9 (beta/1)^(2 beta). Combining the cases proves the proposition with C_eq=9.

snapshot.stmt: Let p^eq(beta,q) = (0, q / beta, 2 q / beta, ..., q). There exists a universal positive constant C_eq (one may take C_eq = 9) such that A_beta(p^eq(beta,q)) <= C_eq (beta / q)^(2 beta) for every integer beta >= 1 and every q in (0,1].

defs.def:amplification-criterion: A_beta(p) = inf_{w in W_beta(p)} (sum_{j=0}^k |w_j|)^2 and M_{beta,k,q} = inf_{p in S_{k,q}} A_beta(p).
defs.def:unbiased-weight-set: W_beta(p) = { w in R^(k+1) : sum_{j=0}^k w_j p_j^0 = 0 and sum_{j=0}^k w_j p_j^ell = 1 for ell = 1, ..., beta }

thm:chebyshev-minimax.proof_tex: Fix c>1, q_max in (0,1), beta>=1, an integer k>=c beta, and q in (0,q_max]. Since k>beta and k is an integer, k>=beta+1. For any p in S_{k,q}, the nodes p_0,...,p_k are distinct and k>=beta, so lem:unbiased-weight-set-nonempty gives W_beta(p) nonempty and lem:amplification-dual-norm applies. Define x_q=2/q-1 and lambda(q)=x_q+sqrt(x_q^2-1). A direct calculation gives q lambda(q)=2-q+2 sqrt(1-q)=(1+sqrt(1-q))^2=rho(q), hence lambda(q)=rho(q)/q. For the lower bound, fix any p in S_{k,q}. Let T_beta be the Chebyshev polynomial and set r_q(u)=T_beta(2u/q-1). This polynomial has degree beta and satisfies |r_q(u)|<=1 on [0,q], so max_j |r_q(p_j)|<=1. By lem:amplification-dual-norm, A_beta(p)^(1/2) >= |r_q(1)-r_q(0)| = |T_beta(x_q)-T_beta(-1)|. The lower part of lem:continuous-chebyshev-endpoint-bound yields |T_beta(x_q)-T_beta(-1)| >= T_beta(x_q)-1 >= c_0(q_max) lambda(q)^beta uniformly for q in (0,q_max]. Therefore A_beta(p) >= c_0(q_max)^2 lambda(q)^(2 beta) = C_-(q_max) (rho(q)/q)^(2 beta), with C_-(q_max)=c_0(q_max)^2. This holds for every schedule p in S_{k,q}. For the upper bound, lem:chebyshev-schedule-admissible gives p^Ch(k,q) in S_{k,q}. Let r be any real polynomial of degree at most beta such that max_{0<=j<=k} |r(p_j^Ch(k,q))| <= 1. Set R(x)=r(q(1+x)/2). Then R has degree at most beta, and because 2 p_j^Ch(k,q)/q - 1 = -cos(pi j/k), max_{0<=j<=k} |R(-cos(pi j/k))| <= 1. By lem:oversampled-chebyshev-lobatto-norming, sup_{x in [-1,1]} |R(x)| <= K(c). Applying lem:continuous-chebyshev-endpoint-bound to R/K(c) gives |r(1)-r(0)| = |R(x_q)-R(-1)| <= K(c) C_0(q_max) lambda(q)^beta. Taking the supremum over all such r and using lem:amplification-dual-norm again gives A_beta(p^Ch(k,q))^(1/2) <= K(c) C_0(q_max) lambda(q)^beta. Hence A_beta(p^Ch(k,q)) <= C_+(c,q_max) (rho(q)/q)^(2 beta) with C_+(c,q_max)=K(c)^2 C_0(q_max)^2. Since the lower bound holds for every p in S_{k,q} and p^Ch(k,q) is admissible, M_{beta,k,q}=inf_{p in S_{k,q}} A_beta(p) is trapped between positive constant multiples of (rho(q)/q)^(2 beta). Constants independent of beta do not affect the pointwise exponential base, so the base is rho(q)/q. As q downarrow 0, rho(q)=(1+sqrt(1-q))^2 tends to 4, so rho(q)/q is asymptotic to 4/q. The equal-spacing benchmark prop:equal-spacing-benchmark exhibits the beta-dependent factor beta/q, whereas the matched upper and lower bounds here identify the beta-independent pointwise minimax base rho(q)/q whenever k>=c beta with c>1. Thus the intrinsic exponent is 2 beta, while the equal-spacing beta/q factor is not the minimax base once the rollout uses more than beta+1 measurement nodes. This theorem is an approximation-theoretic minimax statement about A_beta and M_{beta,k,q}; thm:tv-envelope-design is used separately to translate this amplification minimax into the rollout TV-envelope variance statement. The closest mathematical comparators are fixed-information results: Rivlin1974 supplies the Chebyshev exterior-point extremal inequality, EhlichZeller1964 supplies the Chebyshev-Lobatto mesh norming inequality, and MicchelliRivlin1977 studies optimal recovery from prescribed information. They do not optimize the treated-fraction sampling set p in S_{k,q}, do not impose W_beta(p)-unbiasedness for the causal total treatment effect, and do not target the stochastic total-variation design-variance criterion.

snapshot.stmt: Fix c > 1 and q_max in (0,1), and define the pointwise Chebyshev base rho(q)=(1+sqrt(1-q))^2. There exist positive constants C_-(q_max) and C_+(c,q_max) such that, for every beta>=1, every integer k >= c beta, and every q in (0,q_max], every schedule p in S_{k,q} satisfies A_beta(p) >= C_-(q_max) (rho(q)/q)^(2 beta), while the shifted Chebyshev-Lobatto schedule satisfies A_beta(p^Ch(k,q)) <= C_+(c,q_max) (rho(q)/q)^(2 beta). Hence M_{beta,k,q} has pointwise low-budget exponential base rho(q)/q; as q downarrow 0 this is 4/q, the intrinsic exponent is 2 beta, and the equal-spacing beta/q factor is not minimax in beta once the rollout uses more than beta+1 nodes.

defs.def:chebyshev-schedule: p_j^Ch(k,q) = q { 1 - cos(pi j / k) } / 2 for j = 0, ..., k, and p^Ch(k,q) = (p_0^Ch(k,q), ..., p_k^Ch(k,q)).
defs.def:amplification-criterion: A_beta(p) = inf_{w in W_beta(p)} (sum_{j=0}^k |w_j|)^2 and M_{beta,k,q} = inf_{p in S_{k,q}} A_beta(p).
defs.def:unbiased-weight-set: W_beta(p) = { w in R^(k+1) : sum_{j=0}^k w_j p_j^0 = 0 and sum_{j=0}^k w_j p_j^ell = 1 for ell = 1, ..., beta }

lem:exact-chebyshev-rate-feasible.proof_tex: Fix c>1, beta>=1, k=ceil(c beta), and q in (0,q_max]. Since c>1, k>=beta+1 and hence k>=beta. Since q<=q_max<1, q in (0,1]. By lem:chebyshev-schedule-admissible, p^Ch(k,q) belongs to S_{k,q}. Applying lem:exact-risk-envelope-upper to this schedule gives inf_{w in W_beta(p^Ch(k,q))} sup_{P in P_beta} w' Gamma_P(p^Ch(k,q)) w <= (sigma_0^2/n) A_beta(p^Ch(k,q)). The upper half of thm:chebyshev-minimax gives A_beta(p^Ch(k,q)) <= C_+(c,q_max) (rho(q)/q)^(2 beta). Combining the displays proves the fixed-Chebyshev exact-risk upper bound. Finally, by def:exact-nested-risk, R_exact(beta,k,q) is the infimum of the same criterion over all p in S_{k,q}; since p^Ch(k,q) is admissible, R_exact(beta,k,q) is at most the displayed Chebyshev value.

snapshot.stmt: Fix c>1, beta>=1, k=ceil(c beta), q in (0,q_max], and rho(q)=(1+sqrt(1-q))^2. The admissible shifted Chebyshev-Lobatto schedule satisfies inf_{w in W_beta(p^Ch(k,q))} sup_{P in P_beta} w' Gamma_P(p^Ch(k,q)) w <= (sigma_0^2/n) C_+(c,q_max) (rho(q)/q)^(2 beta). Consequently R_exact(beta,k,q) <= (sigma_0^2/n) C_+(c,q_max) (rho(q)/q)^(2 beta).

defs.def:exact-nested-risk: R_exact(beta,k,q) = inf_{p in S_{k,q}} inf_{w in W_beta(p)} sup_{P in P_beta} w' Gamma_P(p) w.

node.statement: Fix c>1, beta>=1, k=ceil(c beta), q in (0,q_max], and rho(q)=(1+sqrt(1-q))^2. The admissible shifted Chebyshev-Lobatto schedule satisfies inf_{w in W_beta(p^Ch(k,q))} sup_{P in P_beta} w' Gamma_P(p^Ch(k,q)) w <= (sigma_0^2/n) C_+(c,q_max) (rho(q)/q)^(2 beta). Consequently R_exact(beta,k,q) <= (sigma_0^2/n) C_+(c,q_max) (rho(q)/q)^(2 beta).
node.proof_tex: Fix c>1, beta>=1, k=ceil(c beta), and q in (0,q_max]. Since c>1, k>=beta+1 and hence k>=beta. Since q<=q_max<1, q in (0,1]. By lem:chebyshev-schedule-admissible, p^Ch(k,q) belongs to S_{k,q}. Applying lem:exact-risk-envelope-upper to this schedule gives inf_{w in W_beta(p^Ch(k,q))} sup_{P in P_beta} w' Gamma_P(p^Ch(k,q)) w <= (sigma_0^2/n) A_beta(p^Ch(k,q)). The upper half of thm:chebyshev-minimax gives A_beta(p^Ch(k,q)) <= C_+(c,q_max) (rho(q)/q)^(2 beta). Combining the displays proves the fixed-Chebyshev exact-risk upper bound. Finally, by def:exact-nested-risk, R_exact(beta,k,q) is the infimum of the same criterion over all p in S_{k,q}; since p^Ch(k,q) is admissible, R_exact(beta,k,q) is at most the displayed Chebyshev value.

oeq:exact-nested-minimax.proof_tex: lem:exact-chebyshev-rate-feasible proves that for beta>=1, k=ceil(c beta), q in (0,q_max], and rho(q)=(1+sqrt(1-q))^2, inf_{w in W_beta(p^Ch(k,q))} sup_{P in P_beta} w' Gamma_P(p^Ch(k,q)) w <= (sigma_0^2/n) C_+(c,q_max) (rho(q)/q)^(2 beta), and hence R_exact(beta,k,q) <= (sigma_0^2/n) C_+(c,q_max) (rho(q)/q)^(2 beta). Thus Chebyshev-Lobatto is exact-risk rate-feasible at the TV-envelope minimax exponent and pointwise base; exact optimizer status remains open.

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:low-budget-cap, with beta>=1, k=ceil(c beta), q in (0,q_max], and the admissible Chebyshev schedule p^Ch(k,q) in S_{k,q} (lem:chebyshev-schedule-admissible), does the shifted Chebyshev-Lobatto schedule also solve the exact finite-population nested-rollout minimax problem R_exact(beta,k,q), where Gamma_P(p) is the true covariance matrix of the monotone Bernoulli rollout rather than the total-variation variance envelope? The Chebyshev schedule is rate-feasible for R_exact by thm:chebyshev-minimax (the Chebyshev-schedule amplification upper bound) together with lem:exact-risk-envelope-upper (the envelope-to-exact-risk bridge); exact optimality is open.

defs.def:exact-nested-risk: R_exact(beta,k,q) = inf_{p in S_{k,q}} inf_{w in W_beta(p)} sup_{P in P_beta} w' Gamma_P(p) w.
